subject: Chemistry
q: Which intermolecular force is most responsible in allowing xenon gas to liquefy?
question: Which intermolecular force is most responsible in allowing xenon gas to liquefy?
solution: London dispersion Force (LDF), also known as london forces, or instantaneous dipole-induced dipole forces, is the weakest Vander-waal's force which arises in non-polar molecules due to temporary rearrangement of electrons.

=69.70 °C≃70 °C

Hence answer is 70

subject: Chemistry
q: A $5.25 \%$ solution of a substance is isotonic with a $1.5 \%$ solution of urea $\left(\right.$ molar mass $=60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) in the same solvent. If the densities of both the solutions are assumed to be equal to $1.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$, molar mass of the substance will be
question: A $5.25 \%$ solution of a substance is isotonic with a $1.5 \%$ solution of urea $\left(\right.$ molar mass $=60 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) in the same solvent. If the densities of both the solutions are assumed to be equal to $1.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$, molar mass of the substance will be
solution:
Solutions with the same osmotic pressure are isotonic 
Let the molar mass of the substance be $M$ $\pi_1=\mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{RT}=\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{RT}=\pi_2$
So, $\mathrm{C}_1=\mathrm{C}_2$
As density of the solutions are same
So $\frac{5.25}{M}=\frac{15}{60}$
$M=\frac{5.25 \times 60}{1.5}=210$
Hence (D) is correct

The molar mass of the gas in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is : (Given : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>082</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>atmK</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)

The molar mass of the gas in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is : (Given : <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>082</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>atmK</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:

Mass of liquid =135.5-40=95 g.

Volume of glass vessel =95.0 g0.95 g/ml

=100ml.

∴ PV=WMRT

0.82 atm100×10-3=0.5M0.082250

M=125 g/mol

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following is the wrong assumption of kinetic theory of gases ?
question: Which one of the following is the wrong assumption of kinetic theory of gases ?
options: Momentum and energy always remain conserved.
Pressure is the result of elastic collision of molecules with the container's wall.
Molecules are separated by great distances compared to their sizes.
All the molecules move in straight line between collision and with same velocity.
solution: Molecules move very fast in all directions in a straight line by colliding with each other but with different velocity.

subject: Chemistry
q: A mixture of hydrogen and oxygen contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>%</mo></math> hydrogen by mass when the pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn></math> bar. The partial pressure of hydrogen is bar.
question: A mixture of hydrogen and oxygen contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>%</mo></math> hydrogen by mass when the pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn></math> bar. The partial pressure of hydrogen is bar.
solution:
According to Dalton's law of partial pressures, the total pressure by a mixture of gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of each of the constituent gases. 

Pi=xiPPi= partial pressure of ith component.xi=mole fraction of the ith componentP = total pressure

Let WH2=40 g⇒nH2=402=20

WO2=60 g⇒nO2=6032=158

PH2=2020+158×2.2

=2020+1.875×2.2

=2021.875×2.2

=2.0114

≃2.01bar

subject: Chemistry
q: In the reaction. $2 \mathrm{Al}_{(\mathrm{s})}+6 \mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{s})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{3+}+6 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{g}),}$
question: In the reaction. $2 \mathrm{Al}_{(\mathrm{s})}+6 \mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{s})} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{3+}+6 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{g}),}$
options: $33.6 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~H}_{2(\mathrm{g})}$ is produced regardless of temperature and pressure for every mole $\mathrm{Al}$ that reacts
$67.2 \mathrm{~L~H}_{2(\mathrm{g})}$ at STP is produced for every mole $\mathrm{Al}$ that reacts
$11.2 \mathrm{~H}_{2(\mathrm{g})}$ at STP is produced for every mole $\mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{aq})}$ consumed
solution:
$2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s})+6 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{g})$
For each mole of $\mathrm{HCl}$ reacted, $0.5$ mole of $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas is formed at STP. 
$1$ mole of an ideal gas occupies $22.4$ lit at STP.
Volume of $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas formed at STP per mole of $\mathrm{HCl}$ reacted is $22.4 \times 0.5$ litre.
Hence, (D) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q: At a given temperature <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>,</mo></math> gases <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> are found to deviate from ideal gas behaviour. Their equation of state is given as <math><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Here, <math><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> is the van der Waals constant. Which gas will exhibit steepest increase in the plot of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> (compression factor) vs <math><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
question: At a given temperature <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>,</mo></math> gases <math><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> are found to deviate from ideal gas behaviour. Their equation of state is given as <math><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow></mfrac></math> at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Here, <math><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> is the van der Waals constant. Which gas will exhibit steepest increase in the plot of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></math> (compression factor) vs <math><mi mathvariant="normal">p</mi><mo>?</mo></math>
solution: As the b increases the slope increases. We know that b represents the effective size of the molecules. Since, Xe has the biggest size among given noble gases because on moving down the group size increases due to increase in shell, hence Xe will have the highest slope.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Van der Waal's equation for a gas is stated as,

P=nRTV-nb-anV2.

This equation reduces to the perfect gas equation, P=nRTV When,
question:
Van der Waal's equation for a gas is stated as,

P=nRTV-nb-anV2.

This equation reduces to the perfect gas equation, P=nRTV When,
options: temperature is sufficiently high and pressure is low.
both temperature and pressure are very low.
both temperature and pressure are very high.
temperature is sufficiently low and pressure is high.
solution:
At very high temperatures & low pressures, the characteristic constant behaviour can be understood from virial expansion

Z = PV m RT = 1 + b - a RT 1 V m + b 2 V m 2 + ....

Vm, molar volume large i.e  b 2 V m 2 → o

i.e Third term in virial expansion can be ignored.

In qualitative terms, the real gas will show ideal behaviour when the intermolecular forces of attraction will be negligible which is possible at high temperature and low pressure.
Also  b - a RT → o

i.e.  Z ≈ 1  and gas behaves ideally.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which one of the following graphs is not correct for ideal gas?

d= Density, P= Pressure, T=Temperature
question:
Which one of the following graphs is not correct for ideal gas?

d= Density, P= Pressure, T=Temperature
solution:
For ideal gas

PM = dRT

d=PMR1T

Graph between density versus 1/T should be straight line.

Graph between density versus P should be straight line.

So graph between d Vs T is not straight line.

Hence option II is wrong.

q: An open vessel at <math><msup><mrow><mn>27</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></math> is heated until two fifth of the air (assumed as an ideal gas) in it has escaped from the vessel. question: An open vessel at <math><msup><mrow><mn>27</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>o</mi></mrow></msup><mi>C</mi></math> is heated until two fifth of the air (assumed as an ideal gas) in it has escaped from the vessel. Assuming that the volume of the vessel remains constant, the temperature to which the vessel has heated is:
solution:
n1T1=n2T2

⇒n×300=n-2n5T2

⇒300=35T2

⇒T2=500 K

subject: Chemistry
q:
Arrange the following gases in increasing order of van der Waals constant 'a'
(A) Ar
(B) CH4
(C) H2O
(D) C6H6
Choose the correct option from the following.
question:
Arrange the following gases in increasing order of van der Waals constant 'a'
(A) Ar
(B) CH4
(C) H2O
(D) C6H6
Choose the correct option from the following.
solution:
The van der Waals' constant ′a′ is directly proportional to the intermolecular force of attraction; hence, the gas with greater value of  ′a′ can be most easily liquefied.The values of Vander Waal's constant 'a' for the given gases are:
    Gases                    Vander Waal's Constant 'a'
A. Ar                           1.35 L2 atm mol2
B. CH4                       2.25L2 atm  mol2
C. H2O                       5.536L2 atm mol2
D. C6H6                       18.57L2 atm mol2
∴ Increasing order of 'a' for the given gases
A<B<C<D

subject: Chemistry
q: For gaseous state, if most probable speed is denoted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>*</mo></msup></math>, average speed by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover></math>  and root mean square speed by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, then for many molecules, what is the ratios of these speeds?
question: For gaseous state, if most probable speed is denoted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>*</mo></msup></math>, average speed by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>¯</mo></mover></math>  and root mean square speed by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, then for many molecules, what is the ratios of these speeds?
solution:
C*=2RTM       1.414:1.595:1.732

C-=8πRTM

C=3RTM

2    :  8 3.14    :  3

1   : 1.128   : 1.225

C*: C¯:C=1 : 1.128 : 1.225

question: In Vander Waals equation of state of the gas law, the constant ' $b$ ' is a measure of
solution: In Vander Waals equation ' $b$ ' is the excluded volume i.e. the volume occupied by the molecules

Given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the gas constant in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>JK</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is:
question: <math><mn>0.5</mn></math> moles of gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> moles of gas <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> exert a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> in a container of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1000</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> . Given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the gas constant in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>JK</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is:
solution:
According to the ideal gas equation:

PV=nRT

200×10=(0.5+x)R×1000

2=0.5R+xR

x=2-0.5RR=4-R2R

subject: Chemistry
q: Among the following, the incorrect statement is:
question: Among the following, the incorrect statement is:
options: at low pressure, real gases show ideal behaviour.
at very low temperature, real gases show ideal behaviour.
at Boyle's temperature, real gases show ideal behaviour.
at very large volume, real gases show ideal behaviour.
solution: A real gas behaves like ideal gas at high temperature and low pressure. At very low temperature intermolecular forces of attraction will be quite significant as molecular randomness is very low due to which at very low temperature real gases can't show ideal behaviour.

subject: Chemistry
q: For a reaction, $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_{5(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ in a constant volume container, no products were present initially. The final pressure of the system when $50 \%$ of reaction gets completed is
question: For a reaction, $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_{5(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ in a constant volume container, no products were present initially. The final pressure of the system when $50 \%$ of reaction gets completed is
solution: $\begin{gathered}x=\frac{P_0}{2} \\ P_{\text {total }}=P_0-\frac{P_0}{2}+P_0+\frac{P_0}{4}=\frac{7}{4} P_0\end{gathered}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The volume of gas A is twice than that of gas B. The compressibility factor of gas A is thrice than that of gas B at same temperature. What are the pressures of the gases for equal number of moles?
question: The volume of gas A is twice than that of gas B. The compressibility factor of gas A is thrice than that of gas B at same temperature. What are the pressures of the gases for equal number of moles?
solution:
PV=ZnRT

VA=2VB and ZA=3ZB

P=ZnRTV

PAPB=ZAVBZBVA=3ZB×VBZB×2VB=32

2PA=3PB

At constant T and moles; P∝ZV

subject: Chemistry
q:
The volume occupied by 4.75 g of acetylene gas at 50°C and 740 mm Hg pressure is ____________ L. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given R=0.0826 L atm K-1 mol-1]
question:
The volume occupied by 4.75 g of acetylene gas at 50°C and 740 mm Hg pressure is ____________ L. (Rounded off to the nearest integer)

[Given R=0.0826 L atm K-1 mol-1]
solution:
Given mass=4.75 g⇒C2H2g

⇒Moles=4.7526 mol

Temperature=50+273=323 K

P=740760atm

R=0.0826ℓ atmmol K

⇒V=nRTP=4.7526×0.0826×323740760

⇒V=96314.07819240=5.0059ℓ≃5ℓ

subject: Chemistry
q: The compressibility factor for a real gas at high pressure is :
question: The compressibility factor for a real gas at high pressure is :
solution: At high pressure $Z=1+\frac{\mathrm{Pb}}{\mathrm{RT}}$

subject: Chemistry
q: At very high pressures, the compressibility factor of one mole of a gas is given by:
question: At very high pressures, the compressibility factor of one mole of a gas is given by:
solution:
P+aV2V-b=RT

at very high pressure P≫aV2

PV-b=RT

PV-Pb=RT

∴Z=1+PbRT compressibility factor

subject: Chemistry
q: Three bulbs are filled with CH4, CO2 and Ne as shown in the picture. The bulbs are connected through pipes of zero volume. When the stopcocks are opened and the temperature is kept constant throughout, the pressure of the system is found to be______ atm. (Nearest integer).
question: Three bulbs are filled with CH4, CO2 and Ne as shown in the picture. The bulbs are connected through pipes of zero volume. When the stopcocks are opened and the temperature is kept constant throughout, the pressure of the system is found to be______ atm. (Nearest integer).
solution:
The total number of moles remains constant.

n1+n2+n3=nf

n1,n2, n3 moles of gases in the individual containers. nf is resultant moles.

Using ideal gas equation PV=nRT, we van write as 

P1V1RT+P2V2RT+P3V3RT=PfVfRT

P1V1+P2V2+P3V3=PfVf

2×2+4×3+3×4=Pf×9

Pf=289≈3

subject: Chemistry
q:
At the sea level, the dry air mass percentage composition is given as nitrogen gas : 70.0, oxygen gas : 27.0 and argon gas : 3.0. If total pressure is 1.15 atm , then calculate the ratio of followings respectively :
(i) partial pressure of nitrogen gas to partial pressure of oxygen gas
(ii) partial pressure of oxygen gas to partial pressure of argon gas
(Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and Ar are 14, 16, and $40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively)
question:
At the sea level, the dry air mass percentage composition is given as nitrogen gas : 70.0, oxygen gas : 27.0 and argon gas : 3.0. If total pressure is 1.15 atm , then calculate the ratio of followings respectively :
(i) partial pressure of nitrogen gas to partial pressure of oxygen gas
(ii) partial pressure of oxygen gas to partial pressure of argon gas
(Given : Molar mass of $\mathrm{N}, \mathrm{O}$ and Ar are 14, 16, and $40 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively)
solution:
$\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{Po}_2}=\frac{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{N}_2} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}}{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{O}_2} \cdot \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{T}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{N}_2}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}$ {using Dalton's law of partial pressure}
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{70 / 28}{27 / 32}=2.96 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{Ar}}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{O}_2}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{Ar}}}=\frac{27 / 32}{3 / 40}=11.25\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: For an ideal gas, number of moles per litre in terms of its pressure P, gas contant R and temperature T is
question: For an ideal gas, number of moles per litre in terms of its pressure P, gas contant R and temperature T is
solution: PV = nRT (number of moles = n/V) $\therefore$ n/V = P/RT

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is not an assumption of the kinetic theory of gases?
question: Which of the following is not an assumption of the kinetic theory of gases?
options: Collisions of gas particles are perfectly elastic.
A gas consists of many identical particles which are in continual motion.
At high pressure, gas particles are difficult to compress.
solution: At high pressures gas particles are difficult to compress rather they are not compressible at all.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given:



On the basis of data given above, predict which of the following gases shows the least adsorption on a definite amount of charcoal?
question:
Given:



On the basis of data given above, predict which of the following gases shows the least adsorption on a definite amount of charcoal?
solution:
Method 1:
The extent of adsorption is directly proportional to the critical temperature. As H2 has the lowest critical temperature, it will have the lowest extent of adsorption.

Method 2:
The value of adsorption is dependent on the intermolecular forces of attraction. Among the given options, H2 will have the weakest van der Waal's force (London Dispersion), in which only 2 electrons are involved.

Hence, as a force of attraction is the weakest for H2, it will have the lowest adsorption value.

a  and b are vander Waals constants. The correct statement about the gases is:
question:
Consider the following table:
Gas  a/k Pa dm6mol-1 b/dm3mol-1
A 642.32 0.05196
B 155.21 0.04136
C 431.91 0.05196
D 155.21 0.4382

a  and b are vander Waals constants. The correct statement about the gases is:
options: Gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will occupy more volume than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> ; gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be lesser compressible than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>

Gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will occupy lesser volume than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> ; gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be lesser compressible than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>

Gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will occupy more volume than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> ; gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be more compressible than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>

Gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> will occupy lesser volume than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> ; gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></math> will be more compressible than gas <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi></math>
solution:
* Gas A and C have same value of ‘b’ but different value of a so gas having higher value of a have more force of attraction so molecules will be more closer hence occupy less volume.
* Gas B and D have same value of ‘a’ but different value of b so gas having lesser value of b will be more compressible.

subject: Chemistry
q: When does a gas deviate the most from its ideal behavior?
question: When does a gas deviate the most from its ideal behavior?
options: At low pressure and high temperature
At high pressure and high temperature
At low pressure and low temperature
At high pressure and low temperature
solution:
At high pressure & low temperature gas almost near liquefaction range with

(i) - Intense intermolecular forces

(ii) - Volume occupied by molecules is substantial due to low intermolecular distances.

subject: Chemistry
q: If Z is the compressibility factor, then Van der Waal's equation at low pressure can be written as:
question: If Z is the compressibility factor, then Van der Waal's equation at low pressure can be written as:
solution:
Van der Waal's Equation of state for one mole

P + a V 2 V - b = RT

At low pressure, the characteristic constant (a) becomes significant whereas (b) becomes negligible. So (b) is ignored.

P + a V 2 V = RT

PV + a V = RT

PV RT = RT RT - a VRT = 1 - a VRT

At low pressure for most gases z < 1.

subject: Chemistry
q: 'a' and 'b' are van der Waals' constants for gases. Chlorine is more easily liquefied than ethane because
question: 'a' and 'b' are van der Waals' constants for gases. Chlorine is more easily liquefied than ethane because
options: a for $\mathrm{Cl}_2 < $ a for $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$ but b for $\mathrm{Cl}_2>$ b for $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$
a for $\mathrm{Cl}_2>$ a for $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$ but $b$ for $\mathrm{Cl}_2 < $ b for $\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6$
solution: ease of liquefaction $\propto \frac{a}{b}$ for ethane $a=5.49, b=0.0638$ for $\mathrm{Cl}_2 \mathrm{a}=6.49, \mathrm{~b}=0.0562$

subject: Chemistry
q: Two closed bulbs of equal volume V containing an ideal gas initially at pressure pi and temperature T1 are connected through a narrow tube of negligible volume, as shown in the figure below. The temperature of one of the bulbs is then raised to T2. The final pressure Pf is:
question: Two closed bulbs of equal volume V containing an ideal gas initially at pressure pi and temperature T1 are connected through a narrow tube of negligible volume, as shown in the figure below. The temperature of one of the bulbs is then raised to T2. The final pressure Pf is:
solution:
Given, two closed bulbs of equal volumeV containing ideal gas initially of pressure pi  and  temperature T1, which are connected by a narrow tube of negligible volume.

To find the final pressure Pf, when one raised to T2:
Number of moles of gas does not change,
  nTi=nTf
P i V RT 1 + P i V RT 1 = P f V RT 1 + P f V RT 2
2 P i T 1 = P f T 1 + P f T 2 
2PiT1= Pf(T1 + T2)T1 × T2Pf = 2Pi (T2T1+ T2)

subject: Chemistry
q: At 600 K, the root mean square (rms) speed of gas X (molar mass =40) is equal to the most probable speed of gas Y at 90 K. The molar mass of the gas Y is _____ g mol–1. (Nearest integer)
question: At 600 K, the root mean square (rms) speed of gas X (molar mass =40) is equal to the most probable speed of gas Y at 90 K. The molar mass of the gas Y is _____ g mol–1. (Nearest integer)
solution:
The root mean square velocity Vrms=3RTM

The most probable speed Vmp=2RTM

T=Temperature in Kelvin

M=Molar mass

Vrms of XVmp of Y=3TXMY2TYMX

1=3×600×MY2×90×40=MY4

MY=4 gm mol-1

subject: Chemistry
q: Which one of the following is the correct <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>PV</mi></math> vs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> plot at constant temperature for an ideal gas ? ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> stand for pressure and volume of the gas respectively)
question: Which one of the following is the correct <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>PV</mi></math> vs <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> plot at constant temperature for an ideal gas ? ( <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> stand for pressure and volume of the gas respectively)
solution:
From ideal gas equation
PV=nRT [At constant temperature for fixed amount of ideal gas]
PV=constant
So, As P↑⇒PV remain constant.
